heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XCI
card_summary: Campanini's introduction traces the transmission of a pseudo-Aristotelian reference — the "liber de bdellio" (Sefer ha-bedolach, "book of the pearl") — through the medieval Jewish philosophical tradition, ultimately identifying it as a misreading of the title "Theologia" (Theologia Aristotelis) in Arabic manuscripts. Zorzi's immediate source is identified as Shem Tov Ibn Falaquera, with Mosheh Ibn Ezra's Arugat ha-bosem as a parallel conduit, and the Venetian Jewish humanist Yochanan Alemanno proposed as the likely intermediary who combined both traditions. The section then pivots to Section VII, which opens a sustained assessment of Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources, contrasting his approach to that of Johannes Reuchlin: where Reuchlin cites Hebrew texts with philological precision, Zorzi subsumes all Kabbalistic traditions under collective labels (secretiores theologi, sapientes Hebraeorum) in the manner of Ficino's Corpus Hermeticum, treating the Kabbalah as a unified prisca theologia. Chaim Wirszubski's skeptical assessment of the depth of Zorzi's direct acquaintance with genuine Jewish Kabbalah beyond the Zohar and Portae Lucis is quoted.
essay:
# SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO XCI (pp. 91–95): Zorzi's Kabbalistic Sources — Transmission Problems and Methodological Profile

## The "Liber de Bdellio" and Its Genealogy

The chunk opens in medias res, tracing a specific bibliographic puzzle: Zorzi's citation of a pseudo-Aristotelian text he calls the *liber de bdellio*. Campanini demonstrates that this title — "book of the pearl" — is not an authentic Aristotelian or pseudo-Aristotelian work but derives from a layered chain of errors internal to the Arabic-Hebrew transmission of the *Theologia Aristotelis* (itself a medieval Arabic paraphrase of Plotinus's *Enneads* IV–VI, wrongly attributed to Aristotle).

The chain of transmission reconstructed by Campanini runs as follows. The *Theologia Aristotelis* contains a celebrated passage on intellectual ecstasy (*estasi intellettuale*) that became a *locus classicus* in medieval Jewish mystical philosophy. Ibn Falaquera transmitted this passage in his works without specifying its Aristotelian source more precisely than "Aristotele disse." Mosheh Ibn Ezra's *Maqâlat al-hadîqa* (known in Hebrew as *Arugat ha-bosem*, translated by Yehudah al-Charizi) transmitted a shorter version of the same passage attributed to a *Sefer ha-bedolach* — that is, the "book of the pearl" (Hebrew *ha-bedolach*). Paul Fenton, as Campanini notes, demonstrated that this title arose from a misreading of the Arabic *al-lulu* ("pearl"), which in turn was a misreading of *u-thulûjiyâ* (the *Theologia*) as written in Arabic without diacritical points:

> L'ebraico ha-bedolach, infatti, traduce perfettamente l'arabo al-lulu, che doveva essere la lezione che il traduttore si era trovato davanti. A sua volta il termine che ha originato un titolo pseudoaristotelico in arabo, in ebraico e in latino risulta dalla errata lettura del titolo u-(cid:1810)ulû(cid:1026)iyâ, ovvero la Theologia, la cui forma grafica, se scritta in arabo senza punti diacritici, può indurre in errore.

<cite>p. 91–92</cite>

## Yochanan Alemanno as Proposed Intermediary

Campanini identifies a specific problem: Zorzi attributes the passage drawn from Ibn Falaquera to the title *Sefer ha-bedolach*, which derives not from Ibn Falaquera but from Mosheh Ibn Ezra via Yehudah al-Charizi's translation. Since Zorzi does not appear to have known Ibn Ezra's *Arugat ha-bosem* directly, Campanini proposes that the confusion of these two distinct Hebrew sources was mediated by **Yochanan Alemanno** (Giovanni Alemanno), the Venetian Jewish humanist and philosopher who was personally acquainted with Giovanni Pico della Mirandola and other Christian humanists including Paride da Ceresara and possibly Agostino Giustiniani. Alemanno's *Collectanea* (preserved at the Bodleian Library, Oxford) is shown to contain both sources.

> A questo ritratto corrisponde in effetti un personaggio che, contemporaneo di Zorzi, potrebbe avere mediato l'informazione nella forma in cui appare nel De harmonia mundi, mi riferisco a Yochanan Alemanno il quale conosceva entrambe le fonti e le ha ricopiate nella sua Collectanea, conservata alla Biblioteca Bodleiana di Oxford.

<cite>p. 92</cite>

Campanini notes that a direct contact between Alemanno and Zorzi still requires further archival research, but the circumstantial evidence of contemporaneity and shared milieu in Venice is compelling.

## Zorzi's Profile as a Christian Hebraist: A Preliminary Verdict

Closing the broader survey of Zorzi's Hebrew sources, Campanini offers a provisional assessment:

> si delinea la figura di un ebraista cristiano completo, con un preciso programma di rivalutazione delle fonti ebraiche e un bagaglio di conoscenze giudaistiche superiore alla maggior parte dei suoi contemporanei e, senza dubbio alcuno, a quello dei suoi censori.

<cite>p. 92</cite>

Despite his dependence on earlier models and occasional errors, Zorzi emerges as a remarkably learned Christian Hebraist, one whose command of Jewish sources substantially exceeded that of those who later censored him.

## Section VII: Zorzi's Kabbalistic Bibliography and His Reception Method

The chunk transitions to Section VII, which begins with a German epigraph from Gershom Scholem — "Es war eine wahre Feierstunde für mich, als ich auf diese Blätter blickte" — signaling the reverence with which modern Kabbalah scholarship has approached this material.

Campanini frames Zorzi's practice against the precedent of **Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica***, which had set out an explicit list of Kabbalistic sources consulted. Zorzi similarly presents a bibliographic list at the outset of his properly Kabbalistic treatment. However, the two pioneers differ fundamentally in style:

- **Reuchlin** maintains rigorous philological attribution, citing Hebrew texts with precision and in Hebrew throughout his work.
- **Zorzi** subsumes his Kabbalistic sources under generic collective labels: *secretiores theologi*, *theologi Hebraeorum*, *sapientes Hebraeorum*, etc. He treats the entire Kabbalistic tradition as a unified deposit — on the model of Ficino's *Corpus Hermeticum* — forming part of a *prisca theologia* that unites all classical, pagan, and Christian traditions.

> Sulla scorta dei grandi corpora che affascinavano la cultura del Rinascimento a partire dall'opera di Marsilio Ficino, si pensi, per esempio, al Corpus Hermeticum, per Zorzi l'elemento prevalente nelle tradizioni cabbalistiche è la loro sostanziale unità. Le fonti cabbalistiche sono perciò raccolte sotto etichette o diciture che non possono non apparirci generiche: secretiores theologi, theologi Hebraeorum, sapientes Hebraeorum e così via.

<cite>p. 93–94</cite>

This ideological stance — the Kabbalah as a single, coherent *prisca theologia* — makes precise source identification extremely difficult for modern scholars. Zorzi's lost library (which must have been extensive, particularly in Hebraica) compounds the problem further.

## Wirszubski's Assessment and the State of Scholarship

Campanini closes the chunk by quoting **Chaim Wirszubski**'s influential judgment, which is cautious:

> "Francesco Giorgio [...] compiled an impressive list of Kabbalistic books in his De Harmonia Mundi. But he does not say he read them all, and it still remains to be seen how many of them, apart from the Zohar and the Portae Lucis, he had in fact read. Not having attempted to identify his sources, I can only say that the perusal of his commentary leaves me with the impression, for what is worth, that his acquaintance with genuine Jewish Kabbalah falls short of the wealth and variety of Pico's direct sources."

<cite>p. 94–95</cite>

Campanini cites this not as definitive but as evidence that no scholar prior to his own edition has attempted a systematic identification of Zorzi's Kabbalistic sources — a lacuna his introduction aims to fill. The role of distorting intermediaries (converts such as **Flavio Mitridate**, who introduced errors and fabrications into the Latin translations produced for Pico della Mirandola) is also flagged as a structural problem for all Christian Kabbalists of this period, including Zorzi.

## Key Technical Terms and Figures

- **Theologia Aristotelis**: Arabic paraphrase of Plotinus falsely attributed to Aristotle; core conduit of Neoplatonic ecstasy-doctrine into Jewish philosophy.
- **Sefer ha-bedolach** / **liber de bdellio**: "book of the pearl," pseudo-Aristotelian title arising from scribal misreading of Arabic *thulûjiyâ* (Theologia).
- **Ibn Falaquera** (Shem Tov ben Joseph): 13th-century Jewish philosopher, key transmitter of the intellectual-ecstasy passage.
- **Mosheh Ibn Ezra**: 11th–12th c. Andalusian Hebrew poet and philosopher; his *Arugat ha-bosem* the locus of the alternative attribution.
- **Yochanan Alemanno**: Venetian Jewish humanist, proposed missing link between the two textual traditions and Zorzi.
- **Flavio Mitridate**: Convert, Latin translator for Pico, notorious for distortions and fabrications in Kabbalistic texts.
- *Prisca theologia*: The Ficinian concept of a perennial theological wisdom underlying all traditions, which Zorzi applies to Kabbalistic literature.
